user_prompt: Describe Bernoulli’s principle through an airplane wing example in depth.

style_bible.visual_style: clean scientific visualization, semi-realistic airplane wing with visible airflow streamlines and pressure color gradients
style_bible.camera_rules: smooth deliberate moves only when needed; holds are perfectly static for reading
style_bible.lighting_rules: neutral daylight, high contrast between wing, air, and background
style_bible.continuity_rules: airplane wing orientation and airflow direction remain consistent across all shots and clips

clips.clip_script: Introduce the airplane wing and show air dividing above and below as the wing moves forward.

shots.framing: wide side view of an airplane wing in flight, no fuselage or cockpit visible
shots.subject_action: the wing moves steadily forward through calm sky as faint airflow lines approach
shots.continuity_note: airflow lines begin to split at the wing’s leading edge, setting up airflow comparison
shots.lighting: even daylight with subtle highlights on wing surface

shots.framing: medium close-up of the wing cross-section with airflow clearly visible
shots.subject_action: airflow divides, with streamlines starting to curve over the top surface
shots.continuity_note: viewer understands airflow separation before speed differences are emphasized

shots.framing: angled close-up emphasizing the curved top surface of the wing
shots.subject_action: airflow above the wing stretches and accelerates compared to below
shots.continuity_note: FINAL FRAME HANDOFF: close-up of wing with faster airflow above and slower airflow below, frozen in mid-motion

clips.negative_prompt: logos, watermarks, brand marks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit, unreadable tiny text

clips.clip_script: Explain pressure differences caused by airflow speed and show how lift is produced.

shots.framing: matching close-up continuation of the wing from previous clip
shots.subject_action: color gradients appear showing lower pressure above the wing
shots.continuity_note: continues directly from previous frozen airflow state

shots.framing: cross-sectional view of wing with pressure zones clearly labeled by color
shots.subject_action: lower pressure above and higher pressure below remain stable for comparison
shots.continuity_note: pressure difference is clearly established before showing lift force

shots.framing: medium view of the wing with an upward force arrow beneath it
shots.subject_action: an arrow shows upward lift resulting from pressure difference
shots.continuity_note: FINAL FRAME HANDOFF: wing held steady with visible upward lift arrow and stable airflow

clips.negative_prompt: logos, watermarks, brand marks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit, unreadable tiny text

full_script_20s: A fixed airplane wing moves through air. Air splits above and below the wing. The curved top accelerates airflow, lowering pressure. Higher pressure below pushes upward. The pressure difference creates lift, keeping the airplane aloft.
